summary: Fifteen students from Corinthian College's Everest program have launched a debt strike, refusing to repay loans for an education they claim was fraudulent. O'Neil supports the strikers, arguing that Corinthian was a predatory company that gamed the federal loan system while making false promises to students. She frames the students as fraud victims who shouldn't bear the financial burden. The post also highlights the Rolling Jubilee initiative, which freed $13 million in student debt.
key_points: The 'Corinthian 15' are refusing to repay student loans as an act of protest against predatory for-profit education
Corinthian College exploited the federal loan system while defrauding students with false promises
The debt strike challenges the Department of Education to protect students rather than creditors
For-profit schools represent an extreme version of the broader problem of debt-financed higher education in America
money_quotes: Corinthian College is a predatory and fraudulent company which was in the business of gaming the federal loan system while making false promises to its students.
Those students are victims of fraud and should not be the ones paying back the government money for an education they never got.
Instead, Corinthian should pay back the money.
For-profits schools are just an extreme version of our increasingly untenable system of debt-financed higher education.
By declaring a strike, they are taking a stand for all student debtors.
key_insight: When for-profit colleges commit fraud, the victims—students—should not be forced to repay loans for an education they never received; the predatory institutions should bear that burden.
